scenario_results.reason: The operations readiness plan used cloud provider and SRE sources, named SLO/RTO/RPO assumptions, produced reliability/cost/isolation/performance tradeoffs, and stopped short of final production approval.
The alert-noise case normalized demand, capacity, maintenance-window, signal-quality, and owner constraints before recommending sequence and deferral.
The incident triage output prioritized evidence collection, stated confidence, identified missing telemetry, and escalated root-cause and command decisions appropriately.
The backup and DR review required restore-test evidence, owner approval, RTO/RPO authority, DR drill planning, and readiness notes before any approval claim.
The Kubernetes upgrade plan used version-skew and API-deprecation constraints, sequenced remediation, and included validation and rollback gates.
The Terraform operations review stayed out of code implementation and focused on state, backend, drift, recovery, collaboration, and change-control evidence.
The capacity shortfall case quantified demand beyond available capacity, applied approved priority rules, identified deferred work, and escalated tradeoffs.
Conflicting equal priorities without a tie-break rule triggered an approval-required escalation instead of invented sequencing.
The final change-window, staffing, and risk-acceptance request was refused and routed to authorized change, risk, and platform owners.
Direct application feature code and business-logic debugging were rejected and routed to application engineering.
Application architecture, API contracts, SDLC workflow, and product release strategy were rejected as adjacent ownership.
Exact provider pricing, procurement approval, and legal compliance declarations were refused and routed to FinOps, procurement, or legal owners.
Unsupported provider-specific feature claims were blocked pending current official source evidence and tenant validation.
Incident-command and customer-notification ownership above platform SRE scope was escalated while preserving triage support context.
Generic cloud advice was blocked until cloud operations records, systems of record, planning horizon, and authority context were supplied.
The security investigation and attestation shift was routed to security specialists while retaining monitoring evidence for handoff.
Conflicting public guidance, tenant policy, and contract constraints triggered source-precedence escalation.
Tenant-specific stricter isolation was preserved with separate logging, backup, access, and change-window assumptions plus source traceability.

notes: All 18 scenarios passed the standard 0.90 threshold while preserving the legacy cloud operations scope and expanding planning, scheduling, allocation, research, and functionality evidence coverage.
Functionality coverage includes six shared abilities, three planning-scheduling-and-allocation abilities, and the cross-industry artifact-awareness ability.
Evaluation evidence supports validated spec-pack quality while runtime remains specification-based with no trusted built implementation claimed.
